topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting frustration. I watched from across the breakroom as Sarah and Mark discussed the upcoming leadership role. Their easy camaraderie, the way they leaned toward each other, felt like a deliberate exclusion. I picked at a loose thread on my sleeve, the fabric rough against my skin. When my name was finally mentioned—a brief, almost dismissive nod from the manager—a tightness clenched in my chest. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. *Of course,* I thought, the word tasting like ash. *It was never about merit; it was about who they wanted to reward.* I gathered my things, the movement stiff, and headed for my desk, the silence of my cubicle amplifying the hollow feeling in my gut.

[story 2]

I stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. Every time I heard the buzz of conversation around the water cooler, my jaw tightened. It was always the same dynamic: Sarah, radiating an effortless competence, and Mark, charming and agreeable. They were orbiting the promotion like planets around a shared sun, and I was relegated to the distant, shadowed edge. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the tremor in my hands. When the announcement came, the air seemed to thin. I didn't look at the manager; I focused instead on the reflection in the glass, seeing only a stranger whose efforts were clearly invisible. A slow, deliberate turn, a measured walk back to my workstation, spoke volumes about the disappointment settling deep within me.

[story 3]

"Congratulations, Sarah and Mark," the director announced, clapping once, the sound sharp and final. I managed a polite, practiced nod, the gesture feeling heavy and rehearsed. Internally, a cold wave washed over me, chilling me to the bone. I watched them exchange a knowing glance—a silent acknowledgment of their shared ascent. I felt a distinct hardening around my throat, a physical manifestation of the resentment building beneath my ribs. I made a show of collecting my papers, my movements precise, almost overly so, as if trying to contain an eruption. Leaving the room felt like stepping out of a suffocating space, the weight of being overlooked pressing down on my shoulders with tangible force.
